summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Major Complex Financial Proceeding – De facto relationship lasting 19 years and 5 months – Where it is just and equitable to make a property adjustment order – A consideration of contributions made by the parties – Where the husband made substantial initial financial contributions to the pool of assets – Where the wife then, throughout the course of the relationship, made significant contributions as the “asset manager” and homemaker – Where the husband continued to work as a tradesperson for the first 16 years of the relationship – Where some of the conduct of the husband made the wife’s contributions as the “asset manager” more arduous – Competing valuation evidence in relation to two of the properties of the parties – Where the properties in question are ordered to be sold – Where the court assesses that the contributions based entitlements of the parties are equal – A consideration of the evidentiary onus proof in circumstances where one party seeks an adjustment in respect of alleged waste or premature distribution of property – Where there is no adjustment in favour of either party pursuant to section 90SM(5) in respect of current and future circumstances – Justice and equity considered.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HOWARD J

BACKGROUND

The applicant in these de facto property proceedings is Ms Fernand (“the wife”). The wife was born in 1964.
The respondent is the de facto spouse of the applicant. The respondent is Mr Fernand (“the husband”). The husband was born in 1951. The parties never married but for convenience I will use the terms “wife” and “husband” in these reasons for judgment.
The parties met and commenced a relationship during 1998. The parties began to live together in August 2002[1] when the wife moved into the husband’s home at N Street, Suburb O, City E, Queensland.
The parties separated on a final basis on 31 January 2022 when the wife left the parties’ then residence at 2 S Street, City E.[2]
The parties have not been able to agree on how to divide their property.
The parties have one child – Mr P. Mr P was born in 2004.
In April 1998 the husband asked the wife to marry him. The wife did not accept that proposal of marriage – but she made it clear to the husband that she was open to considering this in the future. In about January 2021 the wife changed her surname to “[Fernand]”. She did this after consulting with the husband and with their son, Mr P. The wife says that she changed her surname in order “to honour our bond as a family”. I accept this evidence from the wife. In her affidavit filed 21 May 2025 (“the wife’s trial affidavit”) she stated her occupation as “bookkeeper”. In paragraph 4 of her trial affidavit, the wife explains that she is a finance professional. The wife also gives evidence that she had been working as a professional in paid employment in various industries for approximately 15 years prior to 2001. From 2001, the wife undertook bookkeeping and accounting work for the husband’s business interests. The wife had been assisting the husband with the bookkeeping and accounting for his business in an unofficial capacity prior to 2001.
The husband is a tradesperson. The husband has not actively operated his business or engaged in trade services since 2018.
When the parties commenced their relationship the husband operated his business through a company – Q Pty Ltd.[3] The husband was the sole director and shareholder of Q Pty Ltd throughout the relationship – until 2021 when two “B Class” shares were issued to the wife.

SECTION 90SM

The relevant legislation is the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
The wife’s application and the husband’s cross application are both governed by section 90SM of the Act. Section 90SM of the Act was amended when the Commonwealth Parliament enacted the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) on 10 June 2025. Those amendments are applicable to this litigation. The parties agree on that point. By section 90SM(1) of the Act, in property settlement proceedings after the breakdown of a de facto relationship, the Court may make such order as it considers “appropriate” altering the interests of the parties in the property of the parties.
Whilst the parties do not precisely agree on the length of the de facto relationship – ultimately, they both concede that the parties had a long de facto relationship. The husband states that the parties commenced living together in August 2002. The wife contends that the de facto relationship commenced earlier but, the view that I have formed, is that the date when the parties commenced co-habitation in August 2002 is, more likely than not, the date when the de facto relationship commenced.
In paragraph 4 of his trial affidavit the husband states that the parties separated on 31 January 2022. He also states that the relationship was approximately “19 years in duration”. Paragraph 2.2.3 of the written submissions on behalf of the husband[4] is contradictory and confusing. In that paragraph it is stated that the final separation occurred on 31 January 2022 – but it is also stated in that paragraph that the parties had lived separately under one roof for eight years after 2014. The husband’s own evidence (in paragraph 4 of his trial affidavit) confirms that the relationship was approximately 19 years in duration.
I find that the de facto relationship lasted for 19 years and 5 months. Whatever way one looks at it – it was a long relationship. For completeness – I do not accept the husband’s contention that the parties were separated under one roof for approximately eight years. It is contrary to the weight of the evidence including that the wife changed her surname to “[Fernand]” in January 2021 after consulting with the husband. Further, I note that in 2021 the husband, who was then controlling Q Pty Ltd, issued two “B Class” shares to the wife. In addition, I accept the evidence of the wife in relation to this issue.[5]
Section 90SM(2) of the Act states:
“90SM Alteration of property interests

...
(2) The court must not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.”
Notwithstanding the recent amendments to the Act, the decision of the High Court in Stanford & Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108 (“Stanford”) is still applicable. The parties were in a long de facto relationship. That relationship has now ended.
It is just and equitable in this case to make a property settlement order under section 90SM because there is no longer the common use of property by the husband and the wife.[6]
Both parties agree that it is just and equitable for the Court to make a property settlement order pursuant to section 90SM of the Act. Section 90SM(2) of the Act is satisfied.

SECTION 90SM(3)

The next task to be undertaken by the Court is outlined in section 90SM(3) of the Act:
“90SM Alteration of property interests

...
(3) In considering what order (if any) should be made under this section in property settlement proceedings, the court:
(a) is to identify:
(i) the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them; and

(ii) the existing liabilities of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them;

...”

THE POOL OF ASSETS AND LIABILITIES

It is clear from section 90SM(3)(a) that the Court is to identify the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them, and the existing liabilities of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them.
The rights and the interests that have been identified by the Court must be existing as at the date of the trial. I note the recent decision of the Full Court of this Court in the matter of Shinohara & Shinohara (2025) 71 Fam LR 429 (“Shinohara”), especially at paragraphs [121] and [127].
I agree with the submission made by Mr North SC on behalf of the applicant wife, that the previous practice of including notional assets (known as “addbacks”) or prospective liabilities yet to crystalise in a balance sheet is now inconsistent with the Act and contrary to authority. The decision in Shinohara (by the Full Court) establishes that expenditure, which in the past may have justified the inclusion of an “addback”, should now be dealt with by the Court when considering the matters set out in section 90SM(5) of the Act.[7]

Properties in D Street. City E

The parties agree on the value of most of the real estate to be included in the property pool. There are two main exceptions. The first is a property situated at 2 D Street, City E (“[2 D Street]”). It is owned in equal shares by the husband and a business partner, Mr M.
There is also a property situated at 1 D Street (“[1 D Street]”). Again, the value of this property is in dispute. It is owned by the parties’ self-managed superannuation fund - R Pty Ltd as trustee for The Fernand Superannuation Fund.
The dispute between the experts in relation to the value of the properties situated in D Street is significant.
The single expert engaged by the parties to prepare a valuation in respect of the two properties situated at D Street is Mr F (“[Mr F]”).[8] Mr F’s company is known as “[G Valuers]”. The valuation reports prepared by Mr F are dated 8 July 2024. Those reports are annexed to Mr F’s affidavit filed 27 November 2024 (“[Mr F’s] affidavit”).
The Honourable Justice Baumann made an Order on 11 February 2025 granting leave to the wife to adduce further evidence from her own adversarial expert in respect of the two properties situated at D Street. That adversarial expert is Mr B of C Valuers (“[Mr B]”). Mr B prepared reports dated 16 October 2024 and they are annexed to an affidavit filed on 27 November 2024 (“[Mr B’s] affidavit”).
A summary of the competing evidence before the Court in relation to the valuation of the two properties situated at D Street is as follows:
Valuer
	
2 D Street
	
1 D Street

Mr F
	
$2,900,000[9]
	
$1,300,000[10]

Mr B
	
$5,000,000[11]
	
$3,000,000[12]
The two valuers agree on the principles to be applied when valuing real estate. Both experts have valued the properties by considering what, in their respective opinions, is the “highest and best use” to which this land can be put. Both experts also agree on the definition of “highest and best use” in the following terms:
“the use of an asset that maximises its potential and that is physically possible, legally permissible and financially feasible.”[13]

Mr B on 1 D Street

It is convenient for the Court to consider the valuation of 1 D Street at the outset. At page 42 of Mr B’s affidavit he includes the following paragraph:
“Highest and Best Use

Existing uses.

Alternatively, the land is considered suitable for a more intensive use, i.e. a development site given the residential zone.”
The zoning for 1 D Street is “medium density residential”. The property is currently being used for industrial purposes. Mr B points out that this is not an allowable use but he states that, given the prolonged use, it is considered that there is an existing use right. Mr B did recommend that this should be confirmed by the City E City Council or a Town Planner.[14]
Mr B has valued this property using an “income approach” and a “market approach”. At page 32 of his affidavit Mr B provides details concerning the current rental status for 1 D Street. Mr B refers to two leases but states that both of those leases have expired. The first lessee is a related entity – Q Pty Ltd (“Q Pty Ltd”). The lease expired on 30 June 2021 but Mr B has worked on the basis that Q Pty Ltd continues to pay rent in accordance with the expired lease. The base rental at the commencement of the lease was $1,400 plus GST per month. This equates to $16,800 per annum (plus GST). My attention has not been drawn to any other income in respect of that part of 1 D Street which is rented by Q Pty Ltd. I note at this juncture that Mr F has worked on the basis that there is no rent being received from Q Pty Ltd in relation to that part of 1 D Street.
The other lessee at 1 D Street is T Pty Ltd. The lease to that company expired on 31 May 2022. The base rental at the commencement of that lease was $135,000 plus GST per annum. The evidence indicates that the lessee remains in place and continues to pay rent.
Mr Avery worked on the basis that the total rental income currently being received in respect of 1 D Street is $151,800 (plus GST).
At page 33 of his affidavit Mr B has provided rental evidence in respect of properties that he considers to be comparable to 1 D Street. Mr B refers to seven properties. Under a heading “Fair Rental Determination”, Mr B again refers to the fact that the leases for 1 D Street are expired. He goes on to state that, in his opinion, the leases (to Q Pty Ltd and T Pty Ltd) are below market.
Mr B concludes that a fair rental for 1 D Street is $348,316 plus GST per annum. The assessment undertaken by Mr B and his opinion in relation to the question of a fair rental for 1 D Street is included in his written report and can be found at page 33 of his affidavit.
In coming to his conclusion that a fair rent for 1 D Street is $348,316 plus GST per annum Mr B has, amongst other things, taken into account the location of the premises, the building services available and other pertinent factors.[15] In particular, Mr B only applied his assessment to the ground floor lettable areas of 1 D Street - because the mezzanine areas would require additional refurbishment before they could be leased.[16] Mr B concludes that the value of 1 D Street (on the “income approach”) is $2,960,000.[17]
The “market approach” involves a direct comparison with other sale prices achieved in the area. In this way, Mr B has compared what are, in his opinion, the sale prices achieved by comparable properties to 1 D Street. In carrying out this exercise Mr B considered the sale of twelve different properties. These comparisons are included in his valuation report from page 34 to page 41 of his affidavit. Mr B concluded that the valuation of 1 D Street using the “market approach” was $3,000,000.
In summary then, Mr B, having considered both approaches to the valuation of 1 D Street, concluded that the fair value of the freehold interest in 1 D Street on a GST exclusive basis as at the date of valuation was $3,000,000.
The husband does not accept the valuation opinions provided by Mr B in relation to 1 D Street – whether looking at his assessment using the “income approach” or the “market approach”.[18]

Mr F on 1 D Street

Mr F assessed a fair market rental rate for 1 D Street at approximately $70-$75/m2. Mr B assessed a fair market value for rent that could be received for 1 D Street at $127/m2. This represents a significant dispute between the experts in relation to a fair rental in respect of 1 D Street. It is implicit in the evidence of the two experts that each of them criticises the other in respect of the comparable tenancies relied upon. The Court is not in a position to determine whether Mr B or Mr F have relied on appropriate or reasonably comparable tenancies.
Mr F came to the conclusion that the highest and best use for 1 D Street was its current use and Mr F concluded (at page 217 of his affidavit) that the market value of 1 D Street as at 8 July 2024 was $1,300,000.[19]
When valuing 1 D Street - Mr B took into account the redevelopment potential of that property – having regard to the current zoning (“medium density residential”). Mr North SC cross-examined Mr F in relation to Mr F’s report and his opinion that 1 D Street should not be valued as a potential development site. In cross-examination[20] Mr F stated that the fact that 1 D Street is zoned “medium density residential” was a “prime” consideration when considering the highest and best use of the property.
In his oral testimony, Mr F agreed that it was physically possible to redevelop 1 D Street. Mr F also agreed that it was legally permissible – but he was not persuaded that it was financially feasible to do so and in his reasoning in this regard he referred to (at least in his oral testimony) amongst other things, the nature of the improvements on the property. Mr F’s opinion is that it is not possible to value a property as a residential development project unless the valuer knows what is proposed to be built on the property and, further, Mr F’s opinion is that the valuer would need to be provided with costings - including development costs.[21] Mr F stated his opinion that it is difficult to determine the market value of (I infer) either site (1 D Street or 2 D Street) on the basis that the site will be developed without being provided with development costs and without doing a feasibility study. Other factors to be taken into account would be whether the developer would be able to obtain a development permit for the particular use they have in mind.[22]
It is also implicit in the evidence of the two experts that they are critical of each other in relation to the comparable sales evidence concerning 1 D Street. As I stated earlier, Mr B had regard to twelve different properties that had been sold – and which he considered comparable and helpful when formulating his opinion. Mr F has considered five different properties – in respect of comparable sales relevant to the valuation of 1 D Street. These can be found from page 209 of the affidavit of Mr F. Mr North SC cross-examined Mr F about the comparable sales and Mr F conceded that when he took into account comparable sales – not one of the comparable sales was a block of land that was currently being used for industrial purposes but was zoned residential. The comparable properties considered by Mr F (in relation to 1 D Street) were all zoned industrial and were being put to industrial use.[23] Mr F conceded in his evidence that it would have been a benefit for comparison purposes if he had been able to refer to other land that was being used for industrial purposes – but zoned residential. Mr F stated that there were no recent transactions in that area which included residential zoned land. Mr F stated that there are only a few areas in City E that have a remnant industrial usage but have since been zoned residential and he was not able to source any comparable sales with the residential zoning that were currently being used for industrial purposes.[24] At page 142 of the transcript on 23 July 2025 the evidence of Mr F (relating to 1 D Street) continued:
“Mr North SC: [Mr F], I would suggest to you that, with respect to this property, its potential would be maximised if developed to medium-density residential?

[Mr F]: If the site was vacant, I would concur with – with that position, but as it probably stands, I – I don’t agree.”
Although it may not be necessarily evident in the written report of Mr F – he has put considerable thought into the question at hand (relating to 1 D Street) – including, trying to find comparable sales of properties with similar current uses but zoned for residential development.

Mr B on 2 D Street

Mr B confirms at page 57 of his affidavit that the zoning for 2 D Street is “Character Residential”. Again, Mr B states that this property is also currently being put to industrial uses – but, under the zoning, this is not considered an allowable use. Mr B stated that, given the prolonged use of the site for industrial purposes, he considered there to be an existing use right.[25]
At page 73 of his affidavit Mr B describes the first tenancy situated at 2 D Street and states that the first lessee is U Pty Ltd (“[U Pty Ltd”). The lease to U Pty Ltd commenced on 1 July 2022 and will expire on 30 June 2027. The base rental at the commencement of the tenancy was $120,000 plus GST per annum.
The second tenant at 2 D Street is V Pty Ltd (“[V Pty Ltd]”). The commencement date of that lease was 1 July 2023 and the lease expired on 30 June 2025. The base rental at commencement was $70,000 plus GST per annum.
At page 75 of his affidavit, Mr B states that the gross rental for U Pty Ltd is $85/m2 and Mr B considers this to be below market rate. Mr B was not able to locate the precise information he needed to assess whether or not there is a fair market rent being paid by the tenant know as V Pty Ltd.
In coming to the conclusion that the rent paid by U Pty Ltd is below market, Mr B looked at comparable rental properties. He looked at twelve properties in total. When reading Mr B’s valuation (at page 75 of his affidavit) one is left with the impression that the rental income currently being received in respect of 2 D Street is below market. But Mr B was not actually in possession of all of the facts. This is apparent from page 75 of his affidavit.
Mr B considered comparable sales evidence. He considered fifteen or sixteen other properties.[26] At page 86 of his affidavit, Mr B stated that, in his opinion, the highest and best use for 2 D Street is as a development site.
On the basis that the highest and best use for 2 D Street is as a development site – Mr B proceeded to value the site using the “market approach” – namely a direct comparison approach.[27] Mr B concluded at pages 87 and 88 of his affidavit that the value of 2 D Street is $5,000,000.

Mr F on 2 D Street

Mr F states at page 134 of his affidavit that 2 D Street is listed on the Environmental Management Register. Mr F also states (at page 136 of his affidavit) that the old warehouse situated at 2 D Street is listed as a place of cultural heritage value under the Town Planning Scheme, though it is not listed on the State Heritage Register. Mr F states that the heritage listing may restrict redevelopment of that section of the lot. It must be the case that Mr F had access to more information than Mr B – because Mr F has stated that there are three tenancies situated at 2 D Street. The third tenant is W Pty Ltd. In addition to the third tenant, there is another lettable area described as “vacant hard stand”.
On the information provided to Mr F and included at page 143 of his affidavit, the current gross rent received in respect of 2 D Street is $267,783.
Mr F then had regard to comparable properties – and considered the leasing evidence available to him in respect of those comparable properties. He came to the conclusion that the current rents being received in respect of 2 D Street are “at parity with market rent, having regard to the age, condition and location of the subject buildings”. [28]
Mr F then considered comparable sales – five in total.
At page 153 of his affidavit, Mr F concluded that the highest and best use of the property situated at 2 D Street is for a continuation of its current use – though the site does have medium term residential redevelopment potential under the current zoning.
Mr F came to the conclusion that whether using the “income approach” or the direct comparison (market) approach – the value of 2 D Street is $2,900,000.[29]

Conclusion in relation to the competing valuations

Both valuers stood by their respective valuations when cross-examined at the trial. At page 146 of the transcript on 23 July 2025, Mr F gave the following evidence concerning 2 D Street:
“Mr North SC: ...So its maximum – its potential is maximised by development within its zoning, current zoning, I suggest?

[Mr F]: Yes, if it – if it was vacant, I – I would agree, but I disagree due to the substantial nature of the improvements.”
Mr F’s evidence concerning 2 D Street is similar to his evidence relating to 1 D Street. Mr North SC went on to ask Mr F about the financial feasibility of redeveloping 2 D Street and asked Mr F about the valuation of the property on the basis that it was suitable for development:[30]
“Mr North SC: I suggest to you financially feasible?

[Mr F]: And as per [1 D Street], it was that component of the definition that caused me to ..... for a continuation of the current use, given that site at [2 D Street] gives the nature of the improvements, it’s listed on the environmental management register as well, so there’s potential ..... there may be some potential issues there. So that was there. It’s that component of the – of the definition where I felt it wasn’t .....

Mr North SC: [Mr F], as I suggested to you in relation to the other property, if his Honour is not persuaded that you’re right as to the highest and best use, not much of your evaluation is of assistance to him, is it?

[Mr F]: I – I wouldn’t agree with that because the underlying land value, the assessed valuation and the underlying land value per square metre I felt sat within market parameters, taking into account that medium-term development potential. But yes. But – but my highest and best use was for a continuation of the current industrial use .....”[31]
Mr B was cross-examined by Mr Savage KC concerning the financial feasibility of redeveloping 2 D Street and Mr B gave the following evidence:[32]
“Mr Savage KC: So anyway, the point really is that none of the things you would normally do in a feasibility study you’ve actually done in respect of either of these two sites?

[Mr B]: So a feasibility would only be done if – you know, depending on its purpose, you know, if there was drawn up plans, yields, you know, done by professionals, town planning advice. I don’t get engaged to, you know, speculate on feasibility outcomes without, you know, at least – yes – architects and quantity surveys involved.”
In re-examination Mr B was further questioned by Mr North SC in relation to the question of the financial feasibility of redeveloping 2 D Street (and 1 D Street). Mr B gave the following evidence:[33]
“Mr North SC: You were assigned ... a task to value this land?

[Mr B]: Yes.

Mr North SC: You determined this – both blocks were – essentially, their highest and best use was for development ?

[Mr B]: Yes.

Mr North SC: ...consistent with the zoning. In order to arrive at that conclusion, did you feel that your professional obligations obliged you to undertake a feasibility study?

[Mr B]: No.

Mr North SC: And can you explain why?

[Mr B]: Yes. So [2 D Street] has a very low site coverage. Its underlying zoning is residential. It – it does – in my opinion, it – it does not have the capacity to generate an income that matches my opinion of its value as a development site. And it’s primarily due to the quality of the improvements and the – the lack of lettable area of a structure. So that’s what I mean by “site coverage”. I think I’ve commented – just hold on one second. Yes. I can’t seem to find it, but the – the site coverage is extremely low. So I – I – you know, and that’s how we determine it. So if I feel, based on the evidence, the value is higher from a direct comparison or market approach, which is – it’s now called, over the income approach, that is what leads me to make my determination of the highest and best use.

Mr North SC: And with respect to [1 D Street], what led you to that?

[Mr B]: So the structures on [1 D Street] are quite – you know, they are substantial. In my opinion, they can provide income. However, once again, it is a residential-zoned land holding. And subsequently, you know, my approach, my – well, my – both my approaches resulted in a similar figure, slightly different. So on that particular property, I felt, you know, that the structures were good enough for holding income, but it has redevelopment potential given its underlying zoning.”
I was not persuaded by this evidence from Mr B. To begin with I note that Mr B was not aware of all of the tenancies at 2 D Street. Further, given how much is at stake for the parties in the case currently before the Court, I do not consider that the process of reasoning provided by Mr B is explicit. He has not directly answered Mr North SC’s question and, further, Mr B has not clearly enunciated his opinion as to why he believes that the redevelopment of 2 D Street and 1 D Street is financially feasible.
Without the benefit of a some evidence relating to financial feasibility – Mr F did not consider (I infer) that it was either prudent or appropriate to provide a value for either of these parcels of land in D Street solely based on their potential for redevelopment. Mr F placed great emphasis upon the extent of the existing structures on both properties and the amount of rent currently being received by the owners of those two properties.
I am concerned about accepting the evidence of Mr F because, no matter what way one looks at it, the current zoning is for residential development. This applies to both of the parcels of land in D Street. Before the Court could have confidence in accepting the opinion evidence of Mr F concerning the value of the two properties in D Street – it seems to me that he should have provided more detail to explain his opinion that the redevelopment of those two sites was not financially feasible.
It is the case that there has been no financial feasibility study undertaken in respect of either parcel of land at D Street – in relation to the potential redevelopment of one or both of those sites. The experts both agree on the valuation considerations to be applied and they both agree on the definition of “highest and best use” – namely
“the use of an asset that maximises its potential and that is physically possible, legally permissible and financially feasible”.
Mr F’s evidence has been criticised by Mr North SC in the wife’s written submissions filed 20 August 2025 (“the wife’s written submissions”) including in relation to Mr F’s evidence concerning financial feasibility. But my interpretation of Mr F’s evidence is that he was making a reference to “financial feasibility” because that is one of the three components which underpin the definition of “highest and best use”. Reference is made by the wife to the decision of the Full Court GWR v VAR [2006] FamCA 894; (2006) 36 Fam LR 237 (“GWR v VAR”). At paragraph [52] the Full Court referred to the well known decision of Isaacs J in Spencer v Commonwealth (1907) 5 CLR 418 (“Spencer”). The Full Court stated at [52] –
“[52] In our view, the wife’s intention in relation to the property was not relevant to the issue of fact his Honour was required to determine, namely its value. The passage relied upon by senior counsel for the husband in Spencer v Commonwealth [1907] ArgusLawRp 146; (1907) 5 CLR 418 ; 14 ALR 253 is relevant in this context. Also relevant for present purposes is the reference to the valuation of land “for the most advantageous purpose for which it was adapted” (Spencer at CLR 441; ALR 261) which the learned authors, Rost RO & Collins HG, of Land Valuation and Compensation in Australia (3rd ed, Australian Institute of Valuers, 1984) at 90 suggest gave rise to the “principle” which is known widely as “valuation for highest and best use”. The learned authors suggest at 90 that:
Recognition of the willing-selling-willing-buyer concept necessarily involves valuation for the highest and best use for which the land is adapted. The prudent and well-informed vendor (whose existence must be assumed) would not willingly part with his land for a price less than that appropriate to its highest and best use; and the well-informed buyer would not expect to be able to purchase it for less. Each party would take into account “not only the present purpose to which the land is applied, but also any more beneficial purpose to which, in the course of events at no remote period it may be applied, just as an owner might do if he were bargaining with a purchaser in the market. This is the mode in which the land would be valued.” (Isaacs J in the Spencer case.)”
The facts in GWR v VAR were much less complex than the facts in the case currently before the Court. In GWR v VAR the Court was considering one residential allotment of land. A closer consideration of GWR v VAR (including paragraphs [52], [53], [54] and [55]) reveals that the Full Court placed significant reliance upon that part of the decision of Isaacs J in Spencer where his Honour referred to some possible change of purpose to which land could be put and his Honour referred to the requirement of such a change “at no remote period”. More particularly at paragraph [55] in GWR v VAR the Full Court stated, inter alia:
“[55] The requirement of such of subdivision “at no remote period” was also satisfied given the undoubted ability for the subdivision to proceed upon payment of modest sums of money, possibly totalling not more than $2500. The dicta of Isaacs J clearly applies to the B property, whether or not the wife actually formalises its subdivision into two lots at some future stage.”
In GWR v VAR the Court had evidence of what payments would need to be made for the subdivision in that case to proceed. There is a reference to “modest sums of money”. There was no evidence provided to the valuers and there is no evidence before this Court of what sums of money would need to be paid for any redevelopment to proceed in relation to the two properties at D Street currently under consideration.
At page 146 of the transcript on 23 July 2025 Mr F did state that (in relation to both properties) there was “medium term development potential”. So he certainly had this in mind in stating his opinions. But as at the time of trial – the opinion of Mr F was that the highest and best use was for the continuation of the current use.
The conclusion that I have reached is that Mr F was, in his evidence, pointing out the difficulties which confront a valuer in circumstances where there is no evidence whatsoever as to the financial feasibility of redeveloping (for whatever purpose) any particular parcel of land. There is no doubt, from the evidence, that Mr F has decades of experience in valuing land in City E. I have no doubt that, if he considered it to be appropriate, Mr F would have been well able to make any number of assumptions to assist him in his task of providing a value in respect of the relevant properties. It is true that a valuer can make assumptions and provide a valuation based on redevelopment of a parcel of land. In the absence of some information in relation to costings etc. – Mr F was not prepared to make those assumptions. It was within the scope of his remit as a professional valuer to make that decision.
Mr B, on the other hand, was not concerned about making assumptions and he must have done so in order to reach a conclusion that the highest and best use of this land is redevelopment. In his evidence given on 23 July 2025[34] Mr B stated that his reason for concluding that 2 D Street should be valued on the basis of redevelopment is that the land has very low site coverage. He also stated that it is zoned residential. He said it does not have the capacity to generate an income that matches his opinion of the land’s value as a development site. He said this is primarily due to the quality of the improvements and the lack of lettable area of a structure. He said that is why he came to the conclusion that the value would be higher from a direct comparison or “market approach” – ahead of the “income approach”.
In relation to 1 D Street Mr B did not really provide any explanation for his conclusion beyond noting that the land is zoned residential. He even notes that the structures currently situated on 1 D Street are good enough for holding income – but he did say it has redevelopment potential because of the zoning.
As to Mr B’s evidence concerning 1 D Street – I do not consider that he has adequately answered Mr North SC’s question at all.
In relation to both of the properties situated in D Street the conclusion that I have reached is that Mr B has failed to adequately explain how or why it is that redevelopment is financially feasible. I am not saying that Mr B needed to provide precise costings in respect of some specific proposed development. Furthermore, I am not saying that the precise intention of a developer or a precise plan of any proposed development needed to be ascertained but it seems to me that some form of explanation as to why Mr B considers that redevelopment is financially feasible is called for. To borrow wording used by Heydon JA[35] in Makita (Australia) Pty Ltd v Sprowles [2001] NSWCA 305; (2001) 52 NSWLR 705 at [85] - what are “the facts assumed or observed so as to produce the opinion propounded?” Mr B assumes that redevelopment of the land is financially feasible without adequately explaining why that is so. It is unduly hazardous for the Court to begin to try to draw inferences from the evidence of Mr B (such as it is) concerning his conclusions relating to highest and best use. For these reasons I am not placing weight on the opinion evidence of Mr B.
Mr North SC relies upon that passage from the Full Court in GWR v VAR where the Court stated that: “the wife’s intention in relation to the property was not relevant to the issue of fact his Honour was required to determine, namely its value”. I am not interested here as to what any particular person (whether it is one of these parties or some other potential developer) might “intend” to include in a redevelopment. But the Court is interested in satisfying itself that the expert valuers have adequately taken into account and adequately explained for the benefit of the Court – the various components of the definition of highest and best use and, more particularly, that component of the definition which refers to financial feasibility.
On the other hand, Mr F ruled out valuing the land on the basis of redevelopment because he did not think it would be financially feasible – but, again, he failed to explain adequately why redevelopment was not financially feasible.
The accepted principles of valuation require the valuer to ascertain the highest and best use of the land. Before concluding that the highest and best use is for the land to be redeveloped – a valuation will only carry weight if the development of the land is legally permissible, physically possible and financially feasible. Focusing on the question of financial feasibility – the expert valuer is required, in my view, to explain why the redevelopment of the land is financially feasible or why it is not financially feasible.
The valuation of the two D Street properties are very significant issues in this case. The parties could have engaged a quantity surveyor - or some other expert - to support their respective contentions that redevelopment is or is not financially feasible. At the very least there would need to be a better explanation by both of the experts as to the facts that are being assumed to support the conclusion that each has reached. The Court in GWR v VAR was at least aware that the subdivision of the block of land could proceed “upon payment of modest sums of money”. I note that both parcels of land in D Street are already zoned for residential use but the evidence is that council approval for a development would still be required. The sums required for council approval in GWR v VAR were said to be modest – but there is no evidence in this case (to which my attention has been drawn) as to how much will be required for council approval, let alone some estimate of the actual cost of development.
Further, I am not persuaded by the arguments contained in the wife’s written submissions relating to the decision of the High Court in Valuer-General Victoria v WSTI Properties 490 SKR Pty Ltd (2025) 423 ALR 186. I am not convinced that, in the present case, there is an “objective reality” that the buildings currently situated on the two parcels of land in D Street are inhibiting the highest and best use of the land.
Neither of the experts provides a decisive blow that would swing the pendulum in favour of their opinion. The Court is left in an impossible position. Whether my analysis of the evidence and/or the authorities is correct or not – it will make no difference to the Court’s ultimate conclusion in this case. The Court does not know if it is financially feasible or not to redevelop these properties. Neither expert has adequately explained their opinion on this crucial component of highest and best use. The Court is required to make an Order that is just and equitable. Given the vast discrepancy between the two valuations there is significant potential that one of the parties may suffer an injustice if the Court chooses one valuer ahead of the other without some principled reason. There is a long line of authority in this jurisdiction to support the proposition that the Court is not obliged to determine a disputed valuation. In particular I note cases such as In the Marriage of Little (1990) FLC 92-147 and In the Marriage of R H and J W Smith (1991) 15 Fam LR 206 (“In the Marriage of Smith”). In In the Marriage of Smith the Full Court stated, inter alia:
“The determination of values of property is a difficult and uncertain matter at the best of times, but especially in Australia in recent times because there have been frequent and marked variations in the property market. It becomes a difficult and at times hazardous task in s 79 proceedings but which, if performed is likely to have a significant effect for better or worse on the fortunes of the parties.

It is largely for those reasons that a consistent line of authority in this court has emphasised that in cases where a property is to be sold the preferable course is to fix the interests of the parties in the sale price by way of percentages rather than a fixed amount to one party and the balance the other: see, for example, In the Marriage of Waters [1981] FamCA 19; (1981) 6 Fam LR 871 ; [1981] FLC 91-019 , In the Marriage of Docters van Leeuwen [1990] FLC 92-148 and In the Marriage of Little (1990) 14 Fam LR 119 ; [1990] FLC 92-147 .

More relevantly here, where the state of the evidence makes the process of valuation hazardous or uncertain, or where there are wide differences between legitimate valuations because of a volatile market or peculiarities relating to the specific property or otherwise, the ascertainment of value by judicial process may become too uncertain and the preferable course is to order the sale of the property so that its real value can be revealed by market forces. The proceeds of sale are then divided in appropriate proportions between the parties. This is particularly emphasised in Little’s case in the following passage at 123:
Whilst a trial judge should determine a disputed issue of valuation where the evidence enables him to do so, we do not accept that there is an obligation cast upon him to determine such a disputed issue irrespective of the state of the evidence. It may be such that a determination is not possible. In such a case, as in a case where there is a very considerable disparity in the valuation evidence and other evidence indicates that the actual ascertainment of the true value is difficult and complex, the proper solution as between the parties may be to order a sale.
The fact that each of the parties seeks a transfer of that property and neither seeks a sale is not in itself a reason for not adopting that approach: indeed it may emphasise the desirability of doing so in order to avoid the lottery effect which may be involved in choosing between one party and the other.”[36]
These principles were succinctly restated and reaffirmed in Dovgan and Dovgan [2021] FamCA 306 (“Dovgan”) at paragraph [215] per Harper J.
The conclusion I have reached is that, in this particular case, because of the state of the evidence – it is too difficult, too complex, too uncertain and too hazardous[37] for the Court to accept a valuation of the properties situated at D Street – known as 1 D Street and 2 D Street. There is a significant difference between the two valuations relied upon by the parties. Both of the properties must be sold to ensure that the Court is able to do justice and equity between these parties. I am well aware that the husband is not the sole owner of 2 D Street but it is also apparent from the written submissions on behalf of the husband that the husband would prefer to see the properties sold rather than accept some overinflated valuation (to use the words of the husband). I infer from this submission that the husband has the agreement of Mr M. I will not proceed on the basis of that inference though. The matter will have to be relisted so that the Court can give Mr M an opportunity to be heard – unless, of course, the husband is able to provide to the Court a letter confirming that Mr M will agree to the sale of 2 D Street. If it comes to it – it is always possible for the Court to order that the husband’s interest in 2 D Street be sold. My attention has not been drawn to any authority to the contrary.
The Court will also need to give Mr M an opportunity to be heard in relation to the 2012 contract referred to in paragraph 46 of the wife’s written submissions. The husband’s lawyers are required to inform the Court whether or not Mr M wishes to be heard in relation to any issue relevant to the proceedings.
