Contributions

The Full Court of this Court in Shinohara recently reaffirmed the long standing and well-established principles to be applied in the assessment of contributions. At paragraphs [126] and [133] –
“[126] The holistic approach in assessing and determining contributions and adjustments thereto (Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) FLC 93-898 (“Jabour”); Horrigan & Horrigan [2020] FamCAFC 25 (“Horrigan”)) remains applicable. Each of the considerations, by either s 79(4) or s 79(5), requires engagement with the circumstances of the disposal of property, the value it achieved, and its use and application being considered and weighed to achieve the mandate of justice and equity that permeates s 79 of the Act...

...

[133] The proper approach to the assessment of contributions is well established. It is not a mathematical exercise, but rather involves the identification and assessment of all of the parties’ respective contributions, in a holistic way across the course of the relationship and in the post separation period to the point of assessment (Jabour and Horrigan). When engaging in such holistic assessment, all contributions must be weighed collectively and not by way of compartmentalising one against another or the remainder.”
The same principles are applicable to the exercise of power under section 90SM of the Act.
The de facto relationship lasted for 19 years and 5 months. In addition to the length of time of the intact relationship between the wife and the husband – approximately 3.5 years elapsed post separation – up to the time of the trial.
As to the amount of the initial contributions made by each of the parties – a consideration of the submissions made (by both Counsel) provides a snapshot of the manner in which this trial was conducted by both parties. The husband objects to the wife’s evidence which is contained in paragraph 148 of her trial affidavit on the basis that it contains unqualified opinion evidence.
In a similar vein, the wife has objected to the evidence which is contained in paragraph 36 of the husband’s trial affidavit. Again, one of the objections is that the husband is not qualified to give opinion evidence.
The wife maintains that the Court would accept that the husband’s initial contribution was in the order of $2,293,415.[42] It is also submitted (on behalf of the wife) that the Court would accept that her initial financial contribution was in the order of $410,599.
The husband is willing to concede that the wife came to the relationship with a property situated at DD Street, Town EE some shares and superannuation. The husband also conceded that the Town EE property was sold by the wife in 2004 for $205,000.[43]
In paragraph 36 of his trial affidavit the husband estimated that his initial contributions in August 2002 were $3,841,253. By the time of the husband’s written submissions it was the husband’s assertion that his initial contributions were $2,714,368. It is apparent that the husband himself significantly inflated the estimate of his initial contributions. That is surely the only way to account for the reduction of $1.1 million between the affidavit and the submissions. This is yet another example of the husband’s inability to be precise or accurate when it comes to matters relating to money.
In her trial affidavit and in the wife’s written submissions, she has consistently maintained that her initial contribution was $410,599.
Taking into account the concessions made by each party and the assertions made by each party – I find that the initial contributions by the wife were somewhere between approximately $200,000 and $410,599.
Once again, taking into account the concessions made by the parties and the assertions made by each party – I find that the initial contributions made by the husband were somewhere between $2,293,415 and $2,714,368.
In the particular circumstances of this case, the parties failed to put before the Court valuation evidence by qualified experts in relation to properties owned by each of them at the commencement of the de facto relationship. In those circumstances it is only possible for the Court to make the findings referred to above – that is to provide a range for which initial contributions are to be considered rather than pinpointing certain amounts.
I do not find it necessary to rule on the objections raised by either party to which I just referred. Nor do I find it necessary to rule on the other objections raised by the parties in respect of other paragraphs contained in their respective trial affidavits relating to the initial contributions made by each of them. Whatever way one looks at the evidence – the husband’s initial financial contributions were significantly higher than the wife’s initial financial contributions.
The husband’s initial greater contributions are to be given significant weight.
In Jabour v Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) 59 Fam LR 475 (“Jabour”) from [51] to [55] the Full Court stated:
“[51] At [105] the primary judge referred to In the Marriage of L A and E A Pierce (1998) 24 Fam LR 377; (1999) FLC 92-844; [1998] FamCA 74 (Pierce).That case represents the culmination of a line of authorities bearing on the issue of how to weigh an initial contribution against other contributions made by the parties over the course of their relationship.

[52] The suggestion that an initial contribution could be “eroded” or “offset” over time by subsequent contributions made by the other party was first made in Lee Steere and Lee Steere [1985] FamCA 57; (1985) 10 Fam LR 431; (1985) FLC 91-626 in which the Full Court said at Fam LR 443; FLC 80,078:
The longer the duration of the marriage, depending on the quality and extent of her contribution, the more the proportionality of the original contribution is reduced ... the proposition that the strength of a contribution made at the inception of a marriage is eroded, not by the passage of time but by the off‑setting contribution of the other spouse, still holds true.
[53] In In the Marriage of M J and J A Money [1994] FamCA 54; (1994) 17 Fam LR 814 at 816; (1994) FLC 92-485 at 81,054 (Money) Fogarty J further observed that:
the term “off-setting contribution” does not necessarily mean “greater contribution”. It simply reflects the circumstance that the respective contributions of the parties over a long period of marriage may “offset’” the significance which might otherwise be attached to a greater initial contribution by one party...the original contribution should not be carried forward as a mathematical proportion; ultimately, when it comes to the trial such a contribution is one of a number of factors to be considered. The longer the marriage the more likely it is that there will be later factors of significance, and in the ultimate the exercise is to weigh the original contribution with all other, later, factors and those later factors, whether equal or not, may in the circumstances of the individual case reduce the significance of the original contribution.
[54] Finally, in Pierce, after quoting Fogarty J’s remarks in Money, the Full Court said:
28. In our opinion it is not so much a matter of erosion of contribution but a question of what weight is to be attached, in all the circumstances, to the initial contribution. It is necessary to weigh the initial contributions by a party with all other relevant contributions of both the husband and the wife. In considering the weight to be attached to the initial contribution, in this case of the husband, regard must be had to the use made by the parties of that contribution.
[55] Again, consistent with the authorities set out above and those which we discuss below, the import of Pierce is that the weight to be attached to an initial contribution must be assessed against the rubric of all of the contributions, both financial and non‑financial, made by the parties over the course of their relationship.”
The principles to be applied have been well settled in this jurisdiction since at least the time of In the marriage of L A and E A Pierce [1998] FamCA 74; (1998) 24 Fam LR 377. The husband in the present case has made a significantly higher initial contribution. In the assessment of contributions, this fact will be given significant weight. But the weight to be given has to be looked at with the myriad of contributions made by both parties, including financial and non-financial contributions. This de facto relationship lasted for 19 years and 5 months. In Jabour the marriage lasted for 27 years. There is, nonetheless, guidance to be obtained from what the Full Court said in Jabour at [136], where it was stated:
“[136] Whatever was the value of the property at the commencement of the relationship its significance has been largely lost given the myriad of the contributions by each of the parties to their various business ventures, through their employment and care of the family over a long relationship, including the contributions made to the retention of the property which we have discussed above. There is no doubt that they both worked hard and over many years they both contributed to the full extent of their capacity within the roles each took within the marriage. As was said in Wallis at [20] it is important that this miscellany of other s 79(4) factors is not accorded a subsidiary role in the assessment of contributions.”
The Court has already made mention of the initial financial contributions made by the wife. I accept the wife’s evidence that she was the primary homemaker and provided the lion’s share of the care for the parties’ child, Mr P. Indeed, the view that I have formed is that the wife provided practically all of the homemaking and the wife provided practically all of the care for the child, Mr P. The husband did make contributions in respect of homemaking and care for the child. But the wife’s contributions vastly outweighed the husband’s. The wife states in her trial affidavit between paragraph 231 and 239 as follows –
“231. Since [Mr P] was born in [...] 2004 I was his primary carer, and undertook the majority of the parenting duties.

232. I was responsible for caring for [Mr P], including getting him ready in the morning, getting him dressed, making breakfast, taking him to and from school every day, I organised all his school uniforms/books/stationary, organised all of his extra‑curricular and took him to and from those activities and tournaments both in primary and high school, I helped him with his homework, I arranged and took him to all his medical appointments, I arranged and organised all his birthday parties, and arranged and facilitated all his social interactions, for example.

233. As an example, in the daily diary notes I refer to and have annexed at “[MRP-22]”, I specifically recorded on 30 April 2013 my afternoon with [Mr P] as follows:

1.15pm 2.45pm collect mail and grocery shop

2.45pm 2.50pm pick up [Mr P] from school

2.50pm 6.00pm take [Mr P] to art & soccer

6.00pm 8.00pm cook tea and help [Mr P] with homework

8.30pm 8.45pm put [Mr P] to bed and read with him

234. In addition to undertaking all parenting tasks, I was solely responsible for the homemaker tasks.

235. We lived on the first floor of [2 S Street], which is approximately 860 sqm of living space. Scaffolding was required to be set up to clean the high walls and ceilings. There were practical impediments with the size of the living space and the low verandah balconies. When [Mr P] was young, I was constantly in a state of stress worrying that he did not fall off a balcony or down the stairs, as I had fallen a couple of times and hurt my knees.

236. I could not physically clean and maintain 860 sqm of living space each week all by myself and up until [Mr P] was about 3 years of age. As such, around 2004 to 2007, I had a cleaning lady for approximately 8-12 hours per week who worked with me to clean the floors, walls, bathrooms, kitchen and appliances, verandahs, stairwells and office area.

237. I was responsible for:
a. grocery shopping (which included walking up and down 30 stairs 5 or 6 times to take the items up from the car to the kitchen);

b. preparing, cooking and cleaning up after all meals;

c. laundering for the family;

d. purchasing and maintaining our clothing and linen for the family;

e. organising our social and family life and holidays;

f. paying household bills and managing our household finances. ;

g. organising household appliance repairs;

h. tidying the clutter;

i. feeding, giving our pets their medication and taking our pets to the vet.
238. Between approximately 2004 and 2007 I was assisted by an “early childhood” student, for 15 to 20 hours per week; she helped me manage the large household and care for [Mr P] and take him to some of his activities - this allowed me time to attend to the extensive business affairs.

239. [Mr Fernand] rarely contributed to any parenting or homemaker tasks and I felt for most of the time I was a single parent, and that I had to be both mother and father.”
I accept this evidence from the wife. The objections to the above evidence are without merit.
In addition to the significant parenting and homemaking contributions made by the wife, the wife, who is a qualified finance professional, worked full time within this family’s group of entities. Between 2001 (prior to the commencement of the de facto relationship) and the time of final separation (January 2022) the wife was entirely responsible for the management of the accounting, administration and bookkeeping work for the eight related entities; two partnerships; and two self-managed super funds; as well as her own and the husband’s personal accounting needs. I note paragraph 215 of the wife’s trial affidavit, and I accept the evidence contained therein. I accept that the husband preferred and requested that the wife complete all of the work for the entities rather than sending the work to an accounting firm because it was beneficial to the family to take advantage of the wife’s knowledge and to save the payment of professional fees. I have also made mention elsewhere in these reasons for judgment to the contributions of this kind (relating to accounting and tax etc) made by the wife post separation.
I have taken into account the fact that, post separation, the wife received an inheritance from her father totalling $880,910.16. This was received in 2022, the same year as the final separation. The wife relied upon that inheritance for the payment of her legal fees, medical expenses and living expenses post separation. The wife did not work post separation between January 2022 until approximately August 2024.[44] In the balance sheet provided by the wife contained in her written submissions, the wife has included money in her own bank account in the sum of $272,977.
The wife has provided extensive evidence in relation to the purchase and sale of real properties during the relationship. I accept the wife’s evidence concerning these issues. I note in particular the wife’s evidence contained between paragraphs 152 and 212 (inclusive) of her trial affidavit. Properties were bought and sold during the relationship in the name of the husband and also by the husband within his partnership with Mr M. The Fernand Superannuation Fund purchased properties as did a partnership formed between the wife and the husband. The husband also purchased a property in State J, in the United States in the name of the entity Fernand Land Company LLC. The husband is the sole member of that entity. Much of the evidence is objected by the husband. But in my view the evidence is admissible and relevant. I agree with the submissions made by Mr North SC – that the evidence is admissible. In particular, parts of the evidence refer to business records and would come under the exception contained in the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth).[45] In any event, it does show that the wife has an integral knowledge of the investments made by the family. I have no doubt that the husband was involved in the decision making as well. But, so far as the management of the bookkeeping, accounting and taxation matters relating to the various properties is concerned – this was left entirely to the wife and she did an admirable job.
The investment properties that were acquired during the relationship generated sufficient income to be self-supporting.[46]
I have already made mention of the accounting, administration and bookkeeping carried out by the wife in respective of the various entities with which the parties were involved – as well as for the husband and the wife personally. The wife was entirely responsible for this over a period of more than twenty years – noting that her work in this regard commenced before the de facto relationship commenced. In respect of any work carried out by the wife prior to the de facto relationship I infer that little or no payment was made for this work. Given the number of years that the wife conducted this work on behalf of the family – the family was able to make significant savings. A very large amount of money would have been paid to outside professionals if the wife had not conducted this work over a long period of time.
From the time of the commencement of the de facto relationship, the husband continued to work as a tradesperson and he earned from Q Pty Ltd income each year in the range of $200,000 to $300,000.[47] The expenses of the family, including the payment of groceries, utilities and school fees, were met by the income derived from the husband from his work as a tradesperson. It is also the case that the husband spent significant sums of money in pursuing his own hobbies including the collection of motor vehicles and other machinery. The husband also travelled on his own to the United States. He was occasionally accompanied by the wife and their son, Mr P. I accept that it was more frequently the case that the husband travelled on his own or with others.[48] I also accept the wife’s evidence that the husband’s travels to the United States added an extra burden on the wife in carrying out her responsibilities with respect to the management and record keeping related to the parties’ entities.[49]
The wife was not paid a regular or a set salary for her accounting and bookkeeping work. However, she did receive income in her name by way of a “wage” or by way of a “dividend” through Q Pty Ltd as part of the family’s tax planning. This varied from year to year.[50] I accept that most of the income received by the wife was mostly applied for the benefit of the family and spent on general and personal expenses. It was also paid to her member’s balance for the Fernand Superannuation Fund, or the wife invested the money into interest bearing cash accounts.
I accept that the wife completed the following types of tasks week in and week out over an approximately twenty-year period. I accept the wife’s evidence contained in paragraph 224 of her trial affidavit. I note in particular that the wife’s tasks included:[51]
“...
Responsible for researching income tax developments and strategies for our business affairs;
Responsible for preparation of periodic and yearend financial statements and income tax adjustments and reconciliations that are reviewed by our tax agents for the family group;
Responsible for quarterly IAS/BAS preparation and lodgement for the family group;
Responsible for the day to day running of operations (Debtors, Creditors, Reconciliations, Payroll for up to 8 employees, liaising with government and industry organisations and customers and suppliers, foreign currency transactions, share transactions, and supervision of staff);
Overseeing enquiries from the ATO and in particular 2 GST Audit in 2011 and 2012;
Pay invoices, business and personal;
Admin work - file and keep document records for all entities;
Liaise with tenants;
Invoice tenants;
All property management tasks, such as arrange repair work, cleaning maintenance, calculating tenant outgoings to be reimbursed, etc;
Meetings with [Mr Fernand], [Mr M] and any relevant third persons (such as accountants, advisors, bank officers, insurance broker, electrical and farm suppliers, [KK Limited]).”
I accept subparagraphs a – k inclusive which are contained within paragraph 224 of the wife’s trial affidavit. As to the amount that the wife may or may not have been paid in respect of those tasks – I have already accepted the wife’s evidence contained earlier in her trial affidavit (including at paragraphs 219 and 220).
I accept the wife’s evidence that, as at the date of separation, the wife’s bank accounts (in her own name) totalled $421,125.73.
After the husband’s retirement, the wife obtained part time employment through FF Financial Services for a period of twelve months. I accept the wife’s evidence that when the wife came home from work (around 6.00pm) she would still be required to cook the evening meal for the family and perform washing and other housekeeping duties – without receiving any notable assistance from the husband.[52]
I accept the evidence of the wife that, since separation, the wife has continued to maintain the books and accounts of some of the parties’ entities and the wife has also continued to manage the investment properties and she has endeavoured to fulfil the statutory obligations of the parties and their entities – in particular in relation to the self-managed super fund. I also accept that on occasions the husband has been less than cooperative. This has made the wife’s tasks more burdensome and arduous. The husband has been uncooperative in respect of 1 S Street. Leaving aside those paragraphs objected to by the husband – there is ample evidence contained in the wife’s trial affidavit between paragraph 258 and 288 for the Court to make the findings to which I have just referred.
Of particular concern to the Court are the difficulties with which the wife has been confronted in respect to the statutory obligations of the self-managed super fund. The husband and the wife are both directors and shareholders of R Pty Ltd, the trustee of the parties’ self-managed super fund.
I note and accept the submissions made by Mr North SC in relation to the obligations of a trustee in respect of a self-managed superannuation fund. Those obligations arise by reason of the provisions of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (Cth) (“the SIS Act”). Pursuant to section 35AE of the SIS Act the trustee of such a fund is obliged to ensure it keeps accounts correctly recorded and explains the transactions and the financial position of the fund. The accounts are to be kept in a way that enables the preparation of the accounts in accordance with section 35B of the SIS Act and so that proper returns can be prepared in accordance with section 35D of the SIS Act.
The wife has provided extensive evidence in relation to this issue and her accountant, Mr GG has also filed two affidavits.
In relation to the objections taken to the wife’s evidence concerning these matters – I have come to the conclusion that I accept the submissions of Mr North SC in relation to the admissibility and relevance of those paragraphs in the wife’s trial affidavit that are pertinent to this issue. I have disregarded those paragraphs in the wife’s trial affidavit about which there is agreement between the parties concerning the objection taken. But as to the balance of the relevant paragraphs the evidence proffered by the wife (including the annexures relied upon) contain relevant, admissible and important evidence for the Court to consider. My views are the same in respect of the evidence of Mr GG[53] – much of which has been objected to by the husband. I accept Mr North SC’s submissions in relation the admissibility of Mr GG’s evidence also. Ultimately these matters then become a question of weight for the Court. I accept that the relevant parts of the wife’s evidence – along with the evidence of Mr GG demonstrates that the wife, as one of the two directors of the trustee of the self-managed super fund attempted diligently to fulfill the statutory obligations of the trustee after the separation between the parties occurred. It is the case that the wife was met by a repeated and sustained failure on the part of the husband to meaningfully assist by providing information or the necessary instructions to enable the statutory obligations to be fulfilled. The wife was put to both work and expense in attempting to secure statutory compliance. I agree with the submissions made by Mr North SC that the objections proffered by the husband are largely misconceived because it is the fact of the dealings and the disputes that is of chief relevance to this Court rather than the truth of whether or not the superannuation fund was noncompliant. I accept that the evidence is relied upon by the wife to establish what occurred between the wife and the husband and how they responded to the auditor and his queries and concerns. The evidence does disclose a course of conduct where the wife attempted to properly respond to the auditor’s queries and it shows that the wife endeavoured to ensure compliance with the “sole purpose” requirements pursuant to section 62 of the SIS Act. The wife endeavoured to provide valuations of assets as required. The evidence does also demonstrate the husband’s lack of regard for the statutory obligations and it does show his neglect with respect to communication – with the wife and the auditor. The evidence does show that the husband failed to communicate effectively or appropriately and it also shows a tendency on the part of the husband to act unilaterally and an unwillingness by him to respond in a timely manner to reasonable queries. I agree with the submission that the wife’s efforts to manage the superannuation fund after the separation of the parties is evidence of her diligence and her attempts have been fraught with difficulty and expense to which she ought not to have been put. It also demonstrates to the Court, as if any further evidence was needed, that throughout the 19 years and 5 months of the de facto relationship it was the wife who bore the responsibility to ensure that all bookkeeping, accounting, taxation and other financial obligations of the parties and their many entities and business interests were complied with on a week to week basis.

Section 90SM(4)(ca) of the Act

When considering the contributions made by the parties the Court is required by section 90SM(4)(ca) to consider the effect of any family violence to which one party has subjected or exposed the other party on the other’s ability to make financial and/or non-financial contributions to the acquisition, conservation or improvement or any of the property of the parties to the de facto relationship. The section also requires the Court to consider the effect of any family violence on the ability of a party to make a contribution in respect of the welfare of the family.
The definition of family violence is contained in section 4AB of the Act. Relevantly, for present purposes the definition provides:
“Definition of family violence etc
(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.

(2) Examples of behaviour that may constitute family violence include (but are not limited to):

...

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

...

(g) economic or financial abuse; or
...”
Section 4AB(2A) provides examples of behaviour which may constitute economic or financial abuse and hence family violence. The list of examples is non-exhaustive. The Full Court of this Court recently considered section 4AB in a case entitled Pickford and Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249; (2024) 70 Fam LR 85.
It is argued, on behalf of the wife in the present case, that the conduct of the husband relating to 1 S Street and the self-managed superannuation fund, comes within the definition of family violence because the husband’s conduct in relation to those two issues amounts to economic or financial abuse.
The husband’s conduct in relation to 1 S Street and the self-managed superannuation fund was rude, obnoxious, unhelpful, uncooperative, short-sighted and petty. But in my assessment, and in the particular circumstances of this case, it does not amount to family violence. It made the wife’s tasks more difficult. This conduct by the husband will be taken into account by the Court when assessing the contributions based entitlements of the parties because the husband’s conduct made the wife’s contributions more arduous. But it does not come within the definition of family violence.
The husband’s derogatory taunts directed at the wife – including goading and belittling the wife and yelling at the wife – as well as unreasonably requiring the wife to account for the use of her time does come within the definition of family violence – in particular section 4AB(2)(d). That sort of conduct by the husband would have made the wife’s contributions more arduous and burdensome. That sort of conduct causes stress, worry and upset to a person. These are matters that will be taken into account when the Court is assessing contributions.
It has been submitted on behalf of the husband that some of the wife’s conduct has also amounted to family violence because, it is said, that the wife unreasonably denied the husband the financial autonomy that the husband would otherwise have had. The argument relates to a dispute between the parties concerning a pension amount to the husband. This is a pension payable via the self-managed superannuation fund. I am not convinced that the wife’s conduct in relation to these matters comes within the definition of family. As will be seen later in these reasons for judgment, the husband did have cash available to him in the post separation period. That cash and cash equivalents ought to have been sufficient for the husband to survive on. I do not consider the husband has been a victim of family violence.

Conclusion in relation to the assessment of contributions

Over the course of this long relationship both the wife and the husband made significant contributions.
The husband’s initial financial contribution was substantial and this contribution has been given significant weight in the Court’s assessment of contributions. Further, certainly up until the time of his retirement, the husband worked in his trade and produced an income which was overwhelmingly used for the benefit of this family. It is the case that the wife performed the vast majority of the homemaking contributions and the parenting contributions. But I do acknowledge that the husband must have provided some contributions in that regard.
The wife, whilst bringing in at the commencement of the relationship a much smaller financial contribution – nonetheless, for more than 20 years the wife has made a very significant contribution to this asset pool and to the family. In particular, I note that the wife commenced performing bookkeeping work for the family before the parties actually moved in together – that is before the commencement of the actual de facto relationship. Further, the wife, after separation has continued to take care of all of the bookkeeping, accounting and taxation matters for the Fernand Superannuation Fund as well as the Fernand Partnership. It is apparent that, after separation, the wife no longer took care of the husband’s personal bookkeeping, accounting and tax matters. During the time of the intact relationship I have no doubt that the wife not only took care of all of those accounting related matters for the husband – but she also (I infer) monitored the family’s expenditure, including the husband’s expenditure. I have made mention of this elsewhere in these reasons for judgment because the conclusion of the Court is that the husband does not monitor his own expenditure and he does not want to. I have already made a number of findings concerning non-financial contributions made by the wife. But, given the broad range of entities, assets and trusts contained within this family’s pool of property – it is very significant to note that the wife was, in the husband’s own words, the “Asset Manager” for this family.[54] Paragraph 228 of the wife’s trial affidavit contains important evidence. Part of that paragraph has been objected to by the husband on the basis that it is irrelevant and contains opinion evidence. Even if I leave aside the words “[Mr Fernand] did not support my wishes” in paragraph 228 – the rest of the paragraph which follows is admissible and relevant. The wife recounts direct conversations between her and the husband. It is clearly admissible and it is clearly relevant. The wife’s work, throughout this relationship, meant that the husband did not have to continually monitor the financial and accounting requirements in respect of all of the parties’ entities; it meant that the husband could devote himself elsewhere (while he was working as an electrician) to his trade, subsequently to his other outside interests. The husband was content, throughout the relationship, to leave the management of the parties’ assets, bookkeeping, accounting and taxation and financial obligations and interests in the hands of the wife. There is no suggestion that the wife hid or kept anything secret from the husband. To the extent that the husband was interested in finding out the answer to any particular financial question – the inference which is open to the Court, and which I draw, is that the wife was able to and did in fact answer his query. I have no doubt that the wife kept the husband informed in relation to all relevant matters relating to these financial issues. But it is very apparent to the Court that the husband was perfectly happy to leave these matters in the hands of the wife – because he certainly did not want to do them himself. The impression that I have formed is that the husband, in fact, did not possess the skills to do this kind of accounting and money management work. I have no doubt the husband was very much involved in relation to decision making when it came to investing. He has a canny eye for that. But, considering the large asset pool and considering the significant number of entities – the wife’s contribution, especially in relation to the preservation of the asset pool is very significant and must sound in a significant finding in respect of the assessment of contributions. As will be seen later in these reasons for judgment, the conclusion reached by the Court, is that the husband is, in fact, a poor money manager and this family has been able to acquire and especially preserve the large asset pool which it now has – due in large part to the contributions made by the wife. As I have just noted, the wife also made contributions in this regard (bookkeeping, accounting etc) prior to the commencement of the de facto relationship and, further, I am mindful of the fact that the wife has continued to make contributions in the 3.5 years following the end of the de facto relationship.[55]
Ultimately, I accept the submission made by Mr North SC on behalf of the wife that the contributions based entitlements of the parties should be assessed at 50/50.
For completeness, I would point out that I agree with the submissions made by Mr North SC concerning “comparable” cases when it comes to the assessment of contributions. The decision of the Full Court in Anson and Meek [2017] FamCAFC 257; (2017) 57 Fam LR 23 is particularly relevant. At paragraph [158] Aldridge and Cleary JJ stated:
In our opinion, the fact that two different judges acting upon the same evidence may properly reach different conclusions greatly diminishes the value of comparable cases. This is especially so in this jurisdiction where there is almost an infinite variation in the rich factual detail that attend both parenting and property cases. The concern is amplified when the Court is proffered just a selection of cases said to be comparable as opposed to an analysis of all cases that could be said to be comparable.
The Court must be cautious when taking into account “comparable” cases on the assessment of contributions. The countless findings, facts and circumstances which arise within a relationship of approximately 20 years are impossible to replicate. Further, I note what the Full Court, in Anson and Meek, stated at paragraph [132] when considering a particular passage of the decision of the High Court in Planet Fisheries Pty Ltd v La Rosa [1968] HCA 62; (1968) 119 CLR 118. The Full Court stated:
In addition to rejecting the concept of establishing a norm or a range, the passages emphasised seem to us to speak directly against the express consideration of comparable judgments at all.
I agree with the submission made by Mr North SC that the exercise of taking into account “comparable” cases is, when considering the question of the contributions based entitlements of parties, misconceived.

Current and future circumstances – section 90SM(5)

Pursuant to section 90SM(3) of the Act the Court is required, when considering what order (if any) should be made under section 90SM in property settlement proceedings, to take into account the considerations relating to current and future circumstances set out in section 90SM(5). All of the matters listed in section 90SM(5) have been considered by the Court in this case and it is important for the Court to highlight some of the more relevant subsections.

Section 90SM(5)(a)

The Court has considered the allegations of family violence and nothing more need be said at this point in time concerning those allegations.

Section 90SM(5)(b)

The wife has included evidence in relation to her state of health. Much of that evidence is subject to objection. But I note that the wife’s evidence contained in paragraphs 438 and 439 of her trial affidavit have not been objected to.
The wife filed a further affidavit 10 July 2025 which was objected to on the grounds that there had been a failure to comply with orders of the Court[56] and “rules”. Although no specific rule was referred to. Noting the objections taken I am not inclined to have regard to that affidavit.
I accept the wife’s evidence that she suffers from osteoarthritis in her knees which affects her mobility. When she has a flare up of her osteoarthritis the wife is limited to bed rest and, in those situations, the wife is restricted in her standing and walking for long periods of time. Walking up and down stairs is also very restricted when the wife is suffering from a flare up in her osteoarthritis. I accept that the wife has suffered from those issues for approximately 13 years. Without taking into account annexure MRP-60[57] it is apparent that the wife has mild to moderate cataracts in both of her eyes and that her vision has deteriorated. I accept that that condition is beginning to impact how the wife sees a computer screen.
There is another item of evidence that is relevant to the question of the wife’s future circumstances. After the final separation the wife was involved in anaccident and suffered personal injury. In late 2024 the wife commenced proceedings for damages for personal injuries in the District Court of Queensland. As at the time of this trial that claim was yet to be determined. Exhibit 1 in the case currently before the Court is the wife’s Statement of Claim in the District Court proceedings, along with another court document known as a “Statement of Loss and Damage”. These documents were tendered into evidence by the husband. I agree with the submission made by Mr North SC of counsel that those documents were tendered without reservation on behalf of the husband and they are evidence to which this Court can have reference. The Statement of Loss and Damage, which forms part of Exhibit 1, does provide a summary of the wife’s current state of health – or at least current as at late 2024. Given that it was the husband who tendered these document into evidence (Exhibit 1) any objection that the husband may now have in respect of the Court having regard to the matters contained therein has been waived.
Having regard to the available evidence – the wife is now a 62 year old woman (61 years of age at the time of the trial). She had relatively recently retired from work. The wife suffers from, at times, painful and restrictive osteoarthritis in her knees. The wife, because of cataracts, is beginning to have trouble working with a computer screen. The wife has been involved in an accident and has suffered personal injury. It is unknown to what extent the wife will be entitled to damages for such personal injury. For approximately 20 years the wife has worked in what was, essentially, a family business – namely, managing all of the accounting, bookkeeping and other tasks related thereto for the Fernand family’s property holdings and business interests. To put it another way, the wife was not employed on the open market. The wife has had some bookkeeping work after separation. I note her evidence in this regard.
The husband is 74 years old. I accept that he suffers from the medical ailments and conditions which he has outlined in his trial affidavit.
The husband has retired from full-time work. Indeed he retired as a tradesperson in 2018. He does not have any prospects of future employment.

Section 90SM(5)(d), (e) and (w)

These particular subsections within section 90SM(5) also require particular mention in the matter currently before the Court. Those subsections state:
90SM Alteration of property interests

...
(5) For the purposes of subparagraph (3)(b)(ii), the court is to take into account the following considerations, so far as they are relevant:
...
(d) the effect of any material wastage, caused intentionally or recklessly by a party to the subject de facto relationship, of property or financial resources of either of the parties to the subject de facto relationship or both of them;

(e) any liabilities incurred by either of the parties to the subject de facto relationship or both of them, including the nature of the liabilities and the circumstances relating to them;

...
(w) any other fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account.
Much of the wife’s evidence concerning these matters is subject to objection. The wife has drawn information from the husband’s documents including bank records obtained by subpoena or disclosure. Her evidence is contained between paragraphs 382 and 394 (inclusive) of her trial affidavit. There are also various annexures prepared by the wife drawn from documents disclosed by the husband or documents produced under subpoena – including the husband’s bank records. The husband objects on the grounds these paragraphs of the wife’s trial affidavit and the annexures attached thereto contain hearsay and opinion evidence or otherwise they amount to no more than submissions or alternatively are irrelevant.
The annexures marked “[MRP-51]” to “MRP-55]” (inclusive) appear to be business records. This inference is at least open to the Court. To the extent that some of the documents are bank statements from the United States – the husband does have investments in that country and to that extent the documents become admissible as an exception to the hearsay rule (section 69 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth)). As to the other paragraphs contained in the wife’s affidavit and as to annexure MRP-50 – I am of the view that those paragraphs and that annexure should be admitted into evidence because they appear to be nothing more than a summary from the documents that have been annexed or otherwise disclosed. The point is – the husband seems to use accounts for the purposes of his property investing and from those same accounts he pays business expenses and he may also draw personal funds. This much is clear from Exhibit 10 – which is the husband’s Commonwealth Bank statement. The question becomes what weight should be given to this evidence of the wife – namely the evidence contained between paragraphs 382 and 394 of her trial affidavit – along with the annexures attached thereto. The conclusion that I have reached is that the wife’s evidence contained in those paragraphs should not be given much weight by the Court. The wife seeks to build a case, to the effect, that the husband has not been able to account for approximately $772,000 and, so the argument runs on behalf of the wife, the Court should conclude that this amount has been wasted by the husband or has been the subject of premature distribution by the husband to himself to the disadvantage of the wife. The wife argues that the Court should make an adjustment under section 90SM(5) in her favour on account of the findings that she seeks.
The difficulty for the wife is that the evidence that she relies upon contains too many “estimates” and a good deal of supposition. For instance, in the table contained in paragraph 386 of the wife’s trial affidavit there are contained no fewer than 5 amounts which are stated to be “estimates”. Of particular note is item 386(b) in the amount of $200,000. I also note item 386(m) where the husband’s HH share portfolio was estimated in the amount of $140,000. The husband gives a reasonable explanation in relation to his share portfolio in paragraph 273 of his trial affidavit.
The crucial figure for the wife is her starting point – namely that the husband, as at the date of separation had at his disposal cash and shares in sum of $1,524,961. But with so many “estimates” contained in that table (the paragraph 386 table) it is not possible for the Court to conclude that it is accurate. The wife has not discharged the onus of establishing the quantum of the amount that was available to the husband at the time of separation. Nor has the wife discharged the onus of establishing how much the husband spent between the time of separation and the time of the trial. In the wife’s submissions there is reference to concessions made by the husband during cross-examination. The concessions referred to were not convincing. The impression I had was that the husband did not really know how much he had spent between the time of separation and the time of the trial. Nor did the husband know the reason why he had made each item of expenditure. The conclusion I have reached is that the husband is a truthful witness. But, the Court has also reached the conclusion, that the husband is a poor manager of money.
The husband was quite lost when it came to the question of finances, expenditure and assets.[58] I note, for instance, the husband’s evidence at pages 50 and 51 of the transcript of 22 July 2025. The husband was being cross-examined at this point about the significant increase in his weekly expenditure between the husband’s first Financial Statement (Exhibit 4) and the husband’s second Financial Statement (Exhibit 5). It is important to keep in mind that these events did not occur long ago. I note the following exchange:
“Mr North SC: So I in the two years, your other expenditure, which is not itemised, has increased by $2,200?

[Mr Fernand]: Yes

Mr North SC: How has that happened?

[Mr Fernand]: I don’t know

Mr North SC: No?

[Mr Fernand]: I had – I did a lot of repairs then - - -

Mr North SC: Really? You reckon - - -?

[Mr Fernand]: - - - on [Motor Vehicle 5], yes, and that. Yes.

Mr North SC: You reckon that would explain it? $2,200 a week?

[Mr Fernand]: No, no. I’m just saying – I’m giving you an explanation for some of it.”
To be precise, in the husband’s first Financial Statement (Exhibit 4) filed on 15 May 2023 he swore that his total personal expenditure per week was $2,502. In the husband’s second Financial Statement (Exhibit 5) filed on 19 May 2025, the husband swore that his total weekly personal expenditure was $4,307. More particularly, item 32 in the husband’s first Financial Statement includes a total of his “all other expenditure” in the amount of $800. In item 32 of his second Financial Statement the husband includes a figure for his “all other expenditure” in the amount of $3,000. That is what Mr North SC was asking the husband. Apart from a reference to repairs on Motor Vehicle 5 – the husband could not explain the significant increase in his weekly expenditure in the two years that elapsed between May 2023 and May 2025. In the husband’s trial affidavit he concedes that he had cash and cash equivalents at separation of $1,271,523.20. This is apparent from paragraphs 260, 261 and 262 of the husband’s trial affidavit. In addition, he received income between the date of separation and the date of the trial. He admitted this in cross-examination. At the commencement of the trial the husband only had $9,000 in the bank. The husband is not required to account for every item of expenditure post separation – but the point is, that his evidence reveals his poor money management skills. I am sure that if the husband knew what he had spent his money on he would have answered Mr North SC in more detail. I could readily understand if the husband was being asked for an explanation about expenditure that had been made long ago. That is not the case here. It is clear to the Court that the husband is a poor money manager.
At page 71 of the transcript on 22 July 2025 the following exchange occurred:
“Mr North SC: So if you add up what she sets out in 386 with 394, you come to $1,974,061 since separation?

[Mr Fernand]: Yes, that seems pretty right.”
The husband did answer in the affirmative but his evidence was unconvincing. He seemed to be all at sea. It is not immediately clear whether he was answering in the affirmative that he did have the benefit of $1,974,061 since separation or whether he was accepting that the arithmetic was correct in certain paragraphs of the wife’s affidavit. It is not the sort of evidence that would convince the Court that the wife has discharged the evidentiary onus of proving how much the husband spent between the time of separation and the time of the trial.
This is an area that is fraught with difficulty for the party seeking to prove that money has been expended in such a way or in such a manner that the Court should take it into account in adjusting property interests. Previously a party would have argued for a notional add-back in respect of what is termed wastage but, since the decision in Shinohara, the issue is now considered in the context of a possible adjustment pursuant to section 90SM(5). The wife does, however, bear an evidentiary burden of putting forward sufficient evidence to enable the court to make an assessment in relation to whether there should be some adjustment in her favour. The Full Court has previously provided some guidance in this regard. In a decision called Pates & Pates [2018] FamCAFC 171, at paragraph 40 the Full Court (per Ryan, Aldridge and Austin JJ), when referring to the expenditure of money in the context of a potential add-back stated:
“40. The wife erroneously inferred the trial judge implied she bore the legal onus to particularise how the husband expended the money. Of course, she had no such onus of proof but, more importantly, the trial judge did not say or imply she did. Neither did the husband bear any such onus, though the wife wrongly submitted he did. The parties only bore the mutual onus of giving one another full and frank disclosure of their financial circumstances, but that onus does not entail an obligation to explain each and every item of expenditure and every bank account transaction after their separation. Each was at liberty to ask the other about such expenditure and transactions, either by interrogation in advance of the trial or during cross-examination at the trial, but in many instances it would be hardly surprising if the interrogated party could not remember such historic detail. Regardless of the difficult forensic challenge it presents, if one party submits for some expenditure by the other party to be added-back as the second party’s notional asset, the first party bears an evidentiary burden of eliciting sufficient evidence to enable the contended addback to be quantified. If that were not so, the add-back amount would be entirely arbitrary, which could never do.”
The decision in Pates is instructive. The principles enunciated in paragraph [40] in Pates related directly to the question of notional addback but those principles are equally applicable to the question of whether expenditure made by one party should lead to an adjustment in favour of the other party pursuant to section 90SM(5) of the Act. Adopting much of the wording used by the Full Court in Pates, the following principles can be outlined in respect of the evidentiary burden in circumstances where one party seeks an adjustment in their favour pursuant to section 90SM(5) on the basis that the other party has engaged in expenditure which is excessive or can be described as wasteful or a premature distribution. Those principles are:
(a) The parties bear a mutual onus of giving one another full and frank disclosure of their financial circumstances;

(b) That onus does not entail an obligation to explain each and every item of expenditure and every bank account transaction after separation;

(c) Each party is at liberty to ask the other party about such expenditure and transactions – either through the process of interrogation before the commencement of a trial or during cross-examination at the trial; and

(d) The party who is pressing for an adjustment in their favour pursuant to section 90SM(5) bears an evidentiary burden of eliciting sufficient evidence to enable the Court to quantify the amount of money expended and sufficient evidence to enable the Court to assess whether that expenditure is reasonable or whether that expenditure is excessive and/or wasteful.

It is the wife who bears the evidentiary burden of eliciting sufficient evidence to support her contention for an adjustment under section 90SM(5) because of the husband’s excessive expenditure.
In Sinha v Sharma [2019] FCCA 3244, His Honour Judge A. Kelly applied the decision in Pates – in particular in relation to the question of the evidentiary onus. His Honour, in essence, cautioned against allowing these types of cases to descend into an ex post facto audit of the parties’ financial affairs.[59] At paragraph 122 of the decision of Sinha v Sharma, where Judge A. Kelly was referencing the decision in Pates, he stated, inter alia, as follows:
“122. ... In dismissing this ground of appeal, the Full Court also recognised that the option of making an add back was not available since the quantum of the sum expended on legal fees was not proved by the evidence. As to this, their Honours observed that it was erroneous to approach the issue upon an assumed onus of proof since the “parties only bore the mutual onus of giving one another full and frank disclosure of their financial circumstances, but that onus does not entail an obligation to explain each and every item of expenditure and every bank account transaction after their separation.” At the same time, and recognising the forensic challenge that it entailed, the Full Court held that a party contending for an add back in respect of some item of expenditure bore the evidentiary burden of eliciting sufficient evidence to enable the add back to be quantified. Absent sufficient particularisation, the ground of challenge failed and the proposed add back was refused.”

(Emphasis in original and footnotes omitted)
As indicated earlier, the problems outlined by the Full Court in Pates[60] and the question of the evidentiary onus outlined in Pates, apply equally to the matter currently before the Court – where the wife is seeking a finding by the Court that there has been more than $700,000 wasted by the husband or that the husband has prematurely distributed this amount to himself – in circumstances where the wife argues that this conduct should lead to an adjustment in her favour under section 90SM(5).
The wife has not discharged the evidentiary onus. The Court is not persuaded to make an adjustment under section 90SM(5) on the basis that the husband has been guilty of waste or that he has made a premature distribution to himself. The presentation of the wife’s case in relation to this aspect of the matter did lack precision. I agree with the submission made by Mr Savage KC to that effect. The husband has not accounted for every dollar that he has spent since separation but nor is he required to.

Conclusion in relation to section 90SM(5)

I have taken into account the ages of the parties and their respective health issues – along with their prospects (or lack of prospects) for future employment. I am not persuaded that there should be an adjustment under section 90SM(5) on account of any of those issues. Both of the parties are getting older. They both have health issues. I have noted that the wife has been in some paid employment since separation – but it is not clear how much longer she will be able to do that given her own evidence (which I accept) that her problem with her eyes is beginning to impact computer screen work.
As to the wife’s argument for an adjustment because of waste or premature distribution – my conclusions have been outlined above. Even if my assessment of that particular matter is incorrect – it will make no difference in the case currently before the Court. Even if some grounds had been adequately proved by the wife in respect of waste or premature distribution – I would not, in the particular circumstances of this case, have been persuaded to make any adjustment beyond 50/50. That is because the Court’s conclusion in this case is that a 50/50 adjustment is just and equitable. Any greater adjustment in favour of the wife (or the husband for that matter) would not, in the circumstances be just and equitable. However, my close consideration of this issue has confirmed the correctness of my conclusion in relation to the assessment of contributions. The husband does not really know what happened to all of the money he had at his disposal between the time of separation and the time of the trial. On the authority of Pates, the parties are not required to precisely account for all of their expenditure after separation.[61] The conclusions I have reached are that, since separation, the husband has shown himself to be a person who has little or no understanding of budgets and a person who displays a lack of financial prudence. The husband may have been very good tradesperson and he had a canny eye for investing, but once the money came through the door – he was not a good money manager. If the wife had not managed the money, the assets, the tax and the accounting matters for this family and it had been left to the husband – the pool of property would likely be significantly less than what it is today. The fact that the husband was not able to explain the dramatic increase in the total of his weekly expenditure between May 2023 and May 2025 confirms the Court’s conclusion that he is indeed a poor money manager. It is also apparent to the Court that the husband does, in fact, have insight into his poor ability as a money manager and his inability to keep a track of his own expenditure. Further, the husband does have insight in relation to his lack of ability relating to bookkeeping matters, accounting matters and taxation matters. This is precisely why the husband handed over the management of the parties’ bookkeeping, accounting, taxation and money management duties and obligations to the wife and this was done from the outset of their relationship. These findings confirm the correctness of the Court’s decision of a 50/50 assessment in respect of contributions – notwithstanding the husband’s much larger initial financial contribution.

Justice and Equity

This was a long de facto relationship. It lasted for 19 years and 5 months. The parties had one child, Mr P, who is now an adult.
The husband made a very significant financial contribution at the commencement of the relationship. Throughout the relationship both parties made contributions as outlined in these reasons for judgment. Taking all those matters into account and, noting the findings made by the Court concerning the current and future circumstances, the conclusion the Court has come to is that an adjustment of the property interests of these parties so that each receives 50 per cent of the net property pool is just and equitable. It is of course the case that the parties, in their presentation of the case, included assets and liabilities of various entities (and their superannuation) in the one pool. This was done for convenience. It is apparent, though, that the ownership of the different assets and liabilities etcetera has been properly delineated by the parties and in these reasons for judgment.
It is the case, that at this point in time, neither the Court nor the parties know what the precise final property pool will be. That is because of the findings made by the Court concerning the expert valuation evidence and the outstanding tax matters. Whilst it is the case that neither party sought that the disputed properties be sold, it is, nonetheless the case, that it always remains open to the Court, to conclude that in order to do justice and equity between the parties, a sale must occur. I have referred to this at length earlier in the reasons for judgment and I have also referred to the relevant authorities. It was well within the contemplation of these parties that the Court could come to a conclusion that the properties at D Street (about which there remains a dispute) may well be ordered to be sold. It is implicit in the written submissions provided by both parties that, despite the fact that they were not keen for it to occur, it remains, as always, a matter for the discretion of the Court as to whether or not a property will be sold. It is for this reason that percentages have to be, and have been, provided by the Court in relation to how the property adjustment should take place.
The valuations of the D Street properties varied greatly. But, even taking that broad range of valuations into account, along with the other assets of the parties and their various interests and their superannuation – leads the Court to conclude that an outcome whereby each party receives 50 per cent of the net property pool of the parties, their superannuation and their other entities, is a just and equitable outcome. This will provide each party with a sufficient amount of capital and income for their retirement.
It is the case that Mr M will be given a further chance to be heard as to the form of any particular order in relation to a sale of 2 D Street.
In addition, I am concerned in relation to the possible future taxation liabilities in respect of these parties. They are matters that will need to be addressed in the final orders.
In the event that the parties can actually agree on the wording and the form of final orders to address all the matters contained in these reasons for judgment – including the sale of the two properties in D Street and the question relating to the taxation issues, and provided that Mr M confirms that he does wish to be heard in relation to any matter – then there will be no need for any further listing of the matter for directions.
The husband filed an application for partial property settlement and he seeks orders in relation to a caveat. I agree with the submissions made by Mr North SC. It is not appropriate in the circumstances of this case for the Court to make any order by way of partial property settlement. Nor is it appropriate to make any other interim order in the absence of an agreement between the parties. What these parties require is for a final orders to be carried into effect at the earliest opportunity.

Concluding remarks

Before this matter went to trial I did not have the long-term case management of the litigation. I do not specifically recall making an order relating to section 102NL of the Act concerning the rules of evidence. The parties have clearly worked on the basis that the rules of evidence are to apply. I have proceeded on that basis even though I have not been able to locate an order or direction to that effect. At the proposed directions hearing (should one occur) I would request that the parties raise this matter with the Court and draw the Court’s attention specifically to any relevant order or direction that may have been made at some stage during this litigation. Further, in any event, it has to be noted that the matter did proceed on the basis that the rules of evidence applied. However, I have only ruled on those items of evidence which I considered expressly required a ruling.
Finally, it is important for the parties to keep in mind that the Court is not required to refer to every fact or argument relied upon by the losing party in relation to any particular issue. The High Court stated in Whisprun v Dixon Pty Ltd [2003] HCA 48; (2003) 200 ALR 447(“Whisprun”) at [62] per Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ (inter alia):
“A judge’s reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge's failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party’s case.”
The decision in Whisprun has been expressly followed by the Full Court of this Court in Eagle & Scarlett (No 2) [2020] FamCAFC 291 at [103] and in Johanson & Johanson [2022] FedCFamC1A 74; (2022) 66 Fam LR 95 at [43].
Further, Mahoney JA, in Housing Commission of New South Wales v Tatmar Pastoral Company Pty Ltd [1983] 3 NSWLR 378 stated at pages 385 and 386, inter alia:
“It is not the duty of the judge to decide every matter which is raised in argument. He may decide a case in a way which does not require the determination of a particular submission: in such a case he may put it aside or, as Lord Scarman said, merely salute it in passing ...”

(Citations omitted)
In these reasons for judgment the Court has not referred to each every argument or fact put forward or referred to by the parties. As can be noted from the above-mentioned authorities the Court is not required to do so.
